title: Converts to Civil Society: Christianity and Political Culture in Hong Kong By Lida V. Nedilsky Baylor University Press. 2014. 227 pages
abstract: I was recently charged with showing a prominent visiting American sociologist around Hong Kong for the day. Having already been hauled up the Peak in a tourist cable car and taken around other popular attractions, he casually remarked to me that “Hong Kong really is just a big shopping mall.” No doubt he was referring to Hong Kong's vibrant commercial retail culture that thrives largely on the tourist dollars of wealthy mainlanders. But this impression of the city is disingenuous. Less than three months later, thousands of university and secondary school students would file into the streets, set up tents, organize public forums on democracy, create works of art that are now being preserved for museums, and take on representatives of one of the most powerful and rapidly growing economies in the world. They organized medical tents, study tents, and cleaning patrols. And this, in fact, was only one recent episode in a long history of mobilizations in a city that has hosted protests against corruption and politically repressive bills, and for minimum-wage legislation, environmental protection and heritage preservation, as well as the rights of migrant workers and sexual minorities. Hong Kong is also home to migrant communities, not just from the Chinese mainland and other parts of Southeast Asia, but also the Indian subcontinent, as well as African countries. Vast, informal “low-end” economies operate out of dilapidated buildings and night markets alongside the high-rise gleaming skyscrapers and shopping malls. Above all, residents continue to search for their own voice in the midst of an uneasy reunification with the Chinese mainland.
